frameworks.name: drift-diffusion
frameworks.one_line: evidence integration over time as the canonical decision strategy

frameworks.name: probability-summation
frameworks.one_line: non-integration strategy that can mimic signs of integration

frameworks.name: signal-detection theory
frameworks.one_line: framing of single-sample decisions without temporal accumulation

core_question: How can one behaviorally distinguish genuine evidence-integration from non-integration strategies that mimic its signatures in perceptual decision tasks?
key_assumptions: commonly accepted behavioral signatures of integration uniquely diagnose integration
integration and non-integration models make separable predictions if the task is designed appropriately
human observers adopt a single coherent decision strategy within a task
manipulations: task-design features that disentangle integration vs non-integration models
motion discrimination stimulus structured to break model degeneracy
comparison of model fits across integration and non-integration generators
key_findings: several commonly accepted signs of integration were also predicted by non-integration strategies
an integration model could fit data generated by non-integration models
a tailored motion discrimination task falsified non-integration in each human subject
prolonged integration was confirmed in all but one subject
interpretations: identifying a decision-maker's strategy from behavior is harder than commonly assumed
carefully designed tasks can disentangle integration from non-integration accounts
limitations_noted: one subject did not show confirmed prolonged integration
